23. What does prefix Un mean?
a. too much
b. forward
c. not
c. not

24. What does prefix Tri mean?
a. two.
c. too much b. three
b. three

25. What does prefix Bi mean?
a. One
b. Two
c. Three
b. Two

26. What does prefix Uni mean?
a. One
b. Two c. three
a. One

27. What does prefix re- mean?
a. Over
c. wrong b. again
b. again

28. What does prefix over- mean?
a. Under or below
c. Too little b. Too much
b. Too much

29. What does prefix mis- mean?
a. wrong
c. backward b. good
a. wrong

31. Select the prefixes that mean UNDER or BELOW.
a. Sub
C. • over b. Above
a. Sub

33. Select the prefixes that mean BENEATH or BELOW.
a. Half or partly
c. wrong b. under
b. Under

34. Select the prefixes that mean FORWARD.
a. Pro
c. again b. before
a. Pro

LeBron James reigns on the basketball court. In one game in 2021, for instance, the legendary Laker racked up 30 points, 11 rebounds, and 10 assists. He was asked in a post-game press conference what had powered up his spectacular performance. James had a straightforward response. "Sleep," he said. Getting a good rest made him "able to come in with some energy and put it on the floor."
James isn't the only one who commends the benefits of slumber. Doctors and scientists say sufficient sleep can improve physical and mental health and enable people to do better in sports, school, work, and just about anything else.
Before they can rise and shine, teens need 8 to 10 hours of
The Right Daily Dose
sleep. Credit: Riska/Getty Images
While the amount of sleep individual people require varies, age is a major consideration. The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) is a U.S. public health agency. It recommends the following daily amounts of sleep:
• 6 to 12 years old: 9 to 12 hours
13 to 18 years old: 8 to 10 hours
• over 18 years old: at least 7 hours
For many people, however, there's a big difference between these numbers and reality. That's especially true of teens.
According to the CDC, almost 80 percent of high school students don't get enough sleep. On average, high school students get only 6.7 hours per night. Reasons include early school schedules, homework, jobs, and activities like sports.
The Benefits of Sleep
The saying "If you snooze, you lose" definitely doesn't apply to health and well-being. Getting plenty of rest has lots of benefits. Sleep restores energy levels. It enables the body to heal itself and recover from everyday damage to muscles and bones. Doctors say it may stave off sickness, helping to prevent some serious diseases as well as common illnesses like colds and flu. It's good for mental health, too. Research shows that a good dose improves mood and reduces stress.
Inadequate sleep has been linked to depression and anxiety.
According to scientists, sleep is also a brain booster. After a good night's rest, people are better able to focus, maintain concentration, and solve problems. All of these things are essential to memory and learning. That's why experts say that if you've got nine precious hours to prepare for an all-important exam, you're better off bedding down early than staying up late studying.
Getting the Best Rest
To sleep well, try these tips from experts.
• Get some sunshine and physical exercise during the day.
•Switch off screens at least 30 minutes before bedtime. The light they give off can signal your brain to stay alert.
• Try to maintain a consistent schedule, going to bed around the same time each night.
• Create an environment that's as dark and quiet as possible. Turn off lights and put electronics in silent mode.
Will getting plenty of sleep grant you the ability to slam dunk the ball, snag rebounds, and block shots like LeBron?
Probably not--after all, there's only one King James. It could, however, give you a chance to be the healthiest, happiest,
and most productive version of yourself. So, make sure to prioritize your sleep and aim for the recommended hours every night.

© How Exercise Affects Sleep
© The Importance of Sleep
© The Sleep Habits of Students
© How Sleep Affects Athletes
The Importance of Sleep

According to the article, how are teens and younger children different?
@ Teens find it easier to get up early than younger children.
© Teens find it easier to switch off screens than younger children
C Teens need less sleep than younger children.
© Teens need more sleep than younger children.
D Teens need different amounts of sleep than younger children. (the recommended hours of sleep for teens and younger children are different)

The article states:
Try to maintain a consistent schedule, going to bed around the same time each night.
Which is the closest synonym for the word consistent?
© different ® regular © hasty © recent
Regular
